coredata.dc:title: Effect of annealing temperature on surface morphology and mechanical properties of sputter-deposited Ti–Ni thin films
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lei
Xie, Chaoying
Wu, Jiansheng
coredata.dc:description: 51.39at.%Ti–48.61at.%Ni thin films were prepared by magnetron sputtering and post-annealed at 450, 500, 550 and 600°C, respectively. Crystallization kinetics of as-deposited Ti–Ni thin films was analyzed using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). The evolution of structure, surface roughness and mechanical properties of Ti–Ni thin film annealed at different temperature were investigated by X-ray diffactometer (XRD), atomic force microscope (AFM) and nanoindention test. The results show that amorphous Ti–Ni thin films were crystallized at 506.3°C with 18.6J/g reaction enthalpy. As annealing temperature increased from 450 to 500°C, crystallization degree of Ti–Ni thin film rose and surface roughness increased as well. As for Ti–Ni thin film annealed above 500°C, Ti2Ni phase precipitated and surface roughness first increased and then decreased with increasing annealing temperature. Moreover, amorphous Ti–Ni thin films are softer than crystalline ones. Microhardness and Young's modulus of crystalline Ti–Ni thin films increased with increasing annealing temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Crystallization kinetics
TiNi thin films
Surface roughness
Mechanical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A DSC thermogram for as-deposited Ti–Ni thin film specimen.
Room temperature XRD patterns of Ti–Ni thin film (a) as-deposited and (b–e) annealed for 1h at (b) 450°C, (c) 500°C, (d) 550°C and (e) 600°C.
3D AFM images of Ti–Ni thin films (a) as-deposited and (b–e) annealed for 1h at (b) 450°C, (c) 500°C, (d) 550°C and (e) 600°C.
Evolution of R q values of Ti–Ni thin films with annealing temperature. The dot line indicates the R q value of as-deposited Ti–Ni thin films.
Representative nanoindentation load–displacement curves for Ti–Ni thin films (a) as-deposited and (b–e) annealed for 1h at (b) 450°C, (c) 500°C, (d) 550°C and (e) 600°C.
Load dependence of (a) microhardness, (b) Young's modulus for as-deposited and annealed Ti–Ni thin films for 1h at 450, 500, 550 and 600°C, respectively.

article.ce:title: Effect of annealing temperature on surface morphology and mechanical properties of sputter-deposited Ti–Ni thin films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Education Ministry for High Temperature Materials and Tests, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 51.39at.%Ti–48.61at.%Ni thin films were prepared by magnetron sputtering and post-annealed at 450, 500, 550 and 600°C, respectively. Crystallization kinetics of as-deposited Ti–Ni thin films was analyzed using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). The evolution of structure, surface roughness and mechanical properties of Ti–Ni thin film annealed at different temperature were investigated by X-ray diffactometer (XRD), atomic force microscope (AFM) and nanoindention test. The results show that amorphous Ti–Ni thin films were crystallized at 506.3°C with 18.6J/g reaction enthalpy. As annealing temperature increased from 450 to 500°C, crystallization degree of Ti–Ni thin film rose and surface roughness increased as well. As for Ti–Ni thin film annealed above 500°C, Ti2Ni phase precipitated and surface roughness first increased and then decreased with increasing annealing temperature. Moreover, amorphous Ti–Ni thin films are softer than crystalline ones. Microhardness and Young's modulus of crystalline Ti–Ni thin films increased with increasing annealing temperature.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decade, Ti–Ni thin films have attracted much attention as intelligent and functional materials for the fabrication of micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS) microactuators. Near equiatomic Ti–Ni thin films can generate large output work and display high frequency responses during thermal cycle in virtue of their unique shape memory effect (SME) [1,2]. Magnetron sputtering technique is a MEMS-compatible deposition method, which has been widely used to fabricate Ti–Ni thin films [3]. It is noted that Ti–Ni thin films sputter-deposited at ambient temperature are often amorphous, thus require post-sputtering crystallizing at elevated temperature to obtain the desired shape memory property [4,5].

It is presently recognized that amorphous Ti–Ni thin films crystallize above 450°C. Preliminary studies suggest that the crystallization temperature and corresponding reaction enthalpy are concerned with the composition [6,7] and substrate [8] of sputter-deposited Ti–Ni thin films. Meanwhile the structural evolution and precipitation during crystallization process of Ti–Ni thin films were also investigated [5,8,9]. Since the crystallization process is a procedure of grains nucleation and growth at elevated temperature, it is inferred that annealing temperature and time will influence the structure and property of Ti–Ni thin films. As for Ti-rich Ti–Ni thin films, the sensitivity of microstructure to annealing temperature was demonstrated by Kawamura et al. [10]. The link between annealing temperature and transformation temperature of sputtered Ti-rich and Ni-rich Ti–Ni thin film was reported by Ishida [11] and Woolman [12], respectively. Nevertheless, it is noticed that the effect of annealing temperature on the mechanical property of Ti–Ni thin film has not been investigated to the present.

In MEMS, mechanical property of materials has an important role on the performance of devices. Characterization of the mechanical properties of MEMS materials has been an active research area during the recent years [13,14]. Ishida et al. first evaluated the mechanical properties, including yield stress, elongation [15] and ductility [16] of sputter-deposited Ti–Ni thin films. Besides Fu et al. presented their researches on residual stress of sputter-deposited Ti–Ni thin films [17,18]. However, to our best knowledge, few researches were reported on microhardness and Young's modulus of sputter-deposited Ti–Ni thin films [19,20], which are also important mechanical parameters for their application in MEMS [21]. In present paper, we studied the evolution of microhardness and Young's modulus of sputtered-deposited Ti–Ni thin film annealed at different temperatures. The effects of annealing temperature on the structure and surface morphology of Ti–Ni thin film were also investigated.

Ti–Ni thin films were deposited onto glass substrates in a carrousel type magnetron sputtering apparatus at an ambient temperature. Pure Ti chips with total sputtering area 10mm×10mm were put on a 51at.%Ti–Ni alloy target to compensate Ti content of as-sputtered Ti–Ni thin film. The target holder was rotated at 10rpm during sputtering to guarantee the homogeneity of composition. The base pressure was lower than 5.0×10−3 mTorr and a presputtering of 30min was performed. The argon pressure was set to 0.5Pa and the RF power was 300W. With this set of parameters, the deposition rate of Ti–Ni thin films is 500nm/h. As-deposited Ti–Ni thin films were post-annealed to obtain crystalline specimens at various temperatures for1h in a vacuum with a base pressure of 10−4 Pa. The annealing temperatures were 450, 500, 550 and 600°C, respectively.

The thickness of sputtered Ti–Ni thin film specimens prepared for the experiment is 4.0μm, as checked with an ALPHA-STEP500 Surface Profilometer. Energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDXA) was used to analyze the chemical composition of the specimens, with the result of 51.39at.%Ti and 48.61at.%Ni. Calorimetric data during crystallization of as-deposited thin films were determined by a differential scanning calorimetry (DSC, CDR-TA) instrument at a heating rate of 40Kmin−1 in a protective gas Ar. X-ray diffraction diffactometer (XRD, D/MAX 2550) and atomic force microscope (AFM, Multimode NanoscopeIIIa) were, respectively, employed to analyze the structure and the surface morphology of Ti–Ni thin film specimens.

Mechanical properties of Ti–Ni thin film specimens were investigated by nanoindention test on a Nanoindentor (NHT 06-0140), with a diamond Berkovich type indenter tip forced into the tested specimen under continuous conditions. The entire indentation procedure is performed with a well-controlled force F while continuously monitoring and measuring the displacement h of the indenter. Such procedure provides an estimation of the microhardness and the Young's modulus of the specimen.

Fig. 1 shows a DSC thermogram for as-deposited Ti–Ni thin film specimen. The heat flow as a function of temperature reveals the crystallization process of amorphous Ti–Ni thin film upon heating. Based on the exothermic peak temperature of DSC curve, it is inferred that as-deposited Ti–Ni thin film specimen is crystallized at 506.3°C. The corresponding reaction enthalpy is 18.6J/g.

Room temperature XRD patterns of Ti–Ni thin film specimens are displayed in Fig. 2 . As for as-deposited films, there is only a broad peak around 2θ =42.5°, which suggests an amorphous structure (Fig. 2(a)). The intensities of the broad peak increase and a few crystalline peaks seemingly appear at 2θ range from 42.5° to 45°, as shown in Fig. 2(b). This feature indicates that Ti–Ni thin films exhibited partial crystallization features after annealing for 1h at 450°C. While annealed at 500°C, Ti–Ni thin films were completely crystallized. According to Fig. 2(c), three obvious diffraction peaks at 2θ =43.8°, 44.3° and 44.8°, respectively, correspond to (002), (111) and (021) lattice orientations of martensite B19’ structure of Ti–Ni thin films. When annealing temperature is above 500°C, the diffraction peaks of Ti2Ni phases appear, implying nonequilibrium precipitation reactions during crystallization of Ti–Ni thin films. Simultaneously the intensities of B19’ peak increase gradually as annealing temperature rises from 500 to 600°C (Fig. 2(d and e)).

Fig. 3 depicts typical three-dimensional representations (5μm×5μm surface morphology by AFM) of Ti–Ni thin films before and after annealing. The as-deposited Ti–Ni thin films presented a smooth surface texture consisting of particles fused together (Fig. 3(a)). While Ti–Ni thin films were annealed at 450°C for 1h, columnar structures grew independently or jointly, building up high mountains and deep valleys on the surface (Fig. 3(b)). As annealing temperature increased, the density and height of columnar structures increased as well. The boundaries of the regions in different height were clearly visible on the surface of Ti–Ni films annealed above 450°C (Fig. 2(c–e)). The formation and development of columnar structures is attributed to the grain nucleation and growth during the crystallization process of as-deposited Ti–Ni thin films [22,23].

Based on standard deviation of the data from AFM images, the root mean square (rms) roughness R q of the specimens quantitatively identify the surface roughness of Ti–Ni thin film. R q of Z values (where Z represents the height of points) within the given area of 25μm2 (based on 5μm×5μm scan images of the specimen) is calculated by the following equation:
R q
=
∑
n = 1
N
( Z n −
Z ¯
)
2
N − 1
where Z
n represents the height of a surface point (signed as n),
Z ¯
equal to the mean height of Z
n in AFM topography and N is the number of the data. R q values of as-deposited and annealed Ti–Ni thin film for 1h at different temperatures are displayed in Fig. 4 . It is clear that crystalline films are much rougher than amorphous ones, which has been proved by Fu and co-workers [24]. As mentioned above, the crystallization of as-deposited Ti–Ni thin films is the process of generation and development of columnar structures. Such process induces the formation of regions in different height on the surface. Consequently as annealing temperature rose from 450 to 550°C, surface roughness of Ti–Ni films increased due to the increasing columnar structures shown in Fig. 3(b–d). It is noted that R q value of Ti–Ni thin film crystallized at 600°C is less than that of Ti–Ni thin film crystallized at 550°C. This feature can also be attributed to the development of columnar structures throughout the crystallization process. When crystallization temperature was raised from 550 to 600°C, the density of columnar structures increased dramatically, as shown in Fig. 3(d and e). In this case, the coalescence of large groups of columnar structures decreased the altitude difference of regions on the surface [22]. Therefore, Ti–Ni thin films crystallized at 600°C tended to be more smooth and homogeneous.

Fig. 5 presents representative nanoindentation load–displacement curves for as-deposited and annealed Ti–Ni thin films at a load of 5, 10, 20mN, respectively. The load–displacement experiments were repeated at five independent locations on the surface of the film. In order to prevent the influence of the substrate, the maximum penetration depth was determined to be less than 400nm, 10% of the film thickness. It was seen that all the data points were almost identical and therefore averaged to determine the mechanical properties of the specimens.

The loading part of nanoindentation load–displacement curve is a combination of elastic and plastic deformation, while the unloading part is mainly the process of elastic deformation [25]. Based on the load–displacement curves, respectively, shown in Fig. 5(a–e), it is found that when as-deposited Ti–Ni thin films annealed, the maximum penetration depth h max at the same load decreased, while the restored depth h r during unloading process increased. Moreover, with the increase of annealing temperature from 450 to 600°C, h max increased and h r decreased gradually on the condition of the same load force.

Based on analysis of load–displacement curves, Young's modulus E and microhardness H of Ti–Ni thin films are deduced by the following equations [19],
H = F / A
with
A = 24.56
h c 2
( for
Berkovich
type
indenter )
E r
=
1 2
π A
d F
d
h c
and
1
E r
=
1 −
ν 2
E
+
1 −
ν
ι
2
E i
where A is the protected contact area, h c the contact depth, dF/dh c the initial slope of the unloading curve, E r a composite modulus of the indenter E i and the specimen material E. ν ι and ν are the Poisson ratio for the indenter and the material, respectively.

Fig. 6 shows load dependence of microhardness, Young's modulus for as-deposited and annealed Ti–Ni thin films for 1h at 450, 500, 550 and 600°C, respectively. It is apparent that as for all the thin film specimens, the evolution of microhardness and Young's modulus with the varying load force was seemingly small, which confirmed that the influence of the substrate on the microhardness and Young's modulus measurement in this study could be negligible [26]. Fig. 6(a) indicates that amorphous Ti–Ni thin films were softer than crystalline ones. This tendency was already observed in sputtered hard thin films, such as W–Si–N [27], W–Ni–N [28], B–C [29] and some bulk metallic glass (BMG) materials [30]. It is probably due to the low compressive stress state and disorder structure of amorphous materials [27,31]. Therefore, when annealing temperature rose from 450 to 500°C, microhardness of Ti–Ni thin films increased with increasing crystallization degree, as analyzed by XRD. While annealing temperature raised from 500 to 600°C, the increment in microhardness can be attributed to the precipitation of Ti2Ni phase, which improves the resistance against plastic deformation of crystalline Ti–Ni thin films [32,33]. The increase of microhardness with rising annealing temperature can also be explained by the effect of the columnar structure, which grew on the surface of crystalline Ti–Ni thin films and increased with the rise of annealing temperature, as investigated by AFM. Such effect has also exhibited in rf-sputtered Cr–B hard thin films [26] and HTS YBCO thin films [34].

With respect to the Young's modulus, the values calculated from load–displacement curves of Ti–Ni thin film specimens were related to the microhardness values. Microhardness increase, Young's modulus increased and vice versa, which is the universal rule for various films [28,29]. The deviance of a few data on the Young's modulus curve shown in Fig. 6(b) may be induced by the error in calculation.

In this study, as-deposited thin films of 51.39at.%Ti and 48.61at.%Ni were amorphous and crystallized at 506.3°C with 18.6J/g reaction enthalpy. With increasing annealing temperature from 450 to 500°C, crystallization degree of Ti–Ni thin film rose and surface roughness increased as well. As for Ti–Ni thin film annealed above 500°C, Ti2Ni phase precipitated and surface roughness first increased and then decreased with the increase of annealing temperature. Amorphous Ti–Ni thin films are softer than crystalline ones. Microhardness and Young's modulus of crystalline Ti–Ni thin films increased with the rising annealing temperature from 450 to 600°C.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Science and Technology Development Foundation of Shanghai (No. 02DJ14042) and Specialized Scientific Research for Doctor Subject in Colleges and Universities (No. 20020248037) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Y. Kawamura, A. Gyobu, H. Horikawa, T. Saburi, J. Phys (France) IV 5 (1995) C8–683.
